text:
APIs allow different software applications to communicate with each other. When working with an API, you typically send HTTP requests to a server and receive a response. Most modern APIs use JSON format for sending and receiving data because it is easy for both humans and machines to read and write.

Authentication is essential for many APIs. You often need an API key or token to verify your identity and ensure you have permission to access the data. Rate limiting is another important concept; it controls how many requests you can make in a given time period to prevent server overload.

Finally, always implement error handling in your code. APIs can return errors for various reasons, such as invalid input or server issues. Proper error handling helps your application respond gracefully to these situations.
